questions.text: What does it mean for MST to be "fully EVM-compatible"?

options.text: MST runs a modified version of Solidity with extra features
MST executes the same EVM bytecode with identical opcodes, precompiles, and gas schedule as Ethereum
MST can import Ethereum blocks directly into its chain
MST uses the same validator set as Ethereum

questions.explanation: Full EVM compatibility means MST's execution layer implements the Ethereum Virtual Machine specification exactly: same opcodes, same precompiled contracts (0x01-0x09), and same gas costs per opcode. A Solidity contract compiles to the same bytecode and behaves identically on both chains.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Full EVM compatibility means MST's execution layer implements the Ethereum Virtual Machine specification exactly: same opcodes, same precompiled contracts (0x01-0x09), and same gas costs per opcode. A Solidity contract compiles to the same bytecode and behaves identically on both chains.

questions.text: Which of the following tools works UNCHANGED on MST due to EVM compatibility?

options.text: Hardhat, ethers.js, and OpenZeppelin Contracts
Truffle, Web3.js, and Solana CLI
Remix, Anchor, and MetaMask
Foundry, Rust, and NEAR SDK

questions.explanation: Hardhat, ethers.js, and OpenZeppelin are Ethereum-native tools that interact with any EVM-compatible chain via standard JSON-RPC. They work on MST with zero modifications. Solana CLI (B), Anchor (C), and NEAR SDK (D) are for non-EVM chains and are irrelevant.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

Hardhat, ethers.js, and OpenZeppelin are Ethereum-native tools that interact with any EVM-compatible chain via standard JSON-RPC. They work on MST with zero modifications. Solana CLI (B), Anchor (C), and NEAR SDK (D) are for non-EVM chains and are irrelevant.

questions.text: A DeFi protocol uses OpenZeppelin's ERC-20 contract and a custom staking contract. They want to deploy on MST. What changes are required in the smart contract code?

options.text: No changes - the contracts deploy as-is on MST
Replace all OpenZeppelin imports with MST-specific libraries
Add a MSTChainId parameter to every function
Recompile the contracts using a special MST Solidity compiler

questions.explanation: Because MST is fully EVM-compatible, standard Solidity contracts compile to the same bytecode. OpenZeppelin contracts are chain-agnostic and work on any EVM chain. No code changes are needed for the contracts themselves - only the deployment RPC endpoint and chain ID in the frontend.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

Because MST is fully EVM-compatible, standard Solidity contracts compile to the same bytecode. OpenZeppelin contracts are chain-agnostic and work on any EVM chain. No code changes are needed for the contracts themselves - only the deployment RPC endpoint and chain ID in the frontend.

questions.text: The precompiled contract at address 0x01 in the EVM provides which functionality?

options.text: SHA-256 hashing
ECDSA public key recovery (ecrecover)
Elliptic curve pairing check
Modular exponentiation

questions.explanation: Precompile 0x01 is ecrecover, which recovers the Ethereum address from a message hash and signature (v, r, s). It's essential for validating signatures in smart contracts (e.g., meta-transactions, multi-sig). MST implements it identically, so any contract relying on signature verification works seamlessly.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Precompile 0x01 is ecrecover, which recovers the Ethereum address from a message hash and signature (v, r, s). It's essential for validating signatures in smart contracts (e.g., meta-transactions, multi-sig). MST implements it identically, so any contract relying on signature verification works seamlessly.

questions.text: Why does MST keep the same gas cost schedule (per opcode) as Ethereum, even though gas is much cheaper?

options.text: To ensure contracts can be audited by Ethereum firms
To preserve security assumptions and anti-spam mechanisms that depend on relative opcode costs
Because the EVM specification cannot be changed
To force developers to optimise for gas

questions.explanation: Many Solidity security patterns rely on the relative cost of opcodes. For example, SSTORE is expensive to prevent storage spam. If MST made it cheap, an attacker could bloat the state. Keeping the same gas schedule ensures that contracts optimised and audited for Ethereum's cost structure remain secure on MST. The absolute price is lower because gas price (MSTC per gas) is lower, not because opcodes are cheaper.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Many Solidity security patterns rely on the relative cost of opcodes. For example, SSTORE is expensive to prevent storage spam. If MST made it cheap, an attacker could bloat the state. Keeping the same gas schedule ensures that contracts optimised and audited for Ethereum's cost structure remain secure on MST. The absolute price is lower because gas price (MSTC per gas) is lower, not because opcodes are cheaper.

questions.text: Consider the following Solidity contract deployed on MST mainnet (chain ID 2001). The contract is identical to one deployed on Ethereum Sepolia (chain ID 11155111). What will the function getChainId() return when called on MST, and why? Explain any difference from the Ethereum deployment.
questions.explanation:
Output on MST: getChainId() returns 2001. isMST() returns true.
        Why: The global variable block.chainid is an EVM opcode (CHAINID) that returns the chain identifier configured in the node. On MST mainnet, the chain ID is 2001. On Ethereum Sepolia, it would be 11155111. The contract itself has no chain-specific code - it simply reads the environment variable. Because MST is fully EVM-compatible, the CHAINID opcode works identically; the only difference is the value returned, which is determined by the node configuration, not the contract bytecode.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Expected Answer & Marks Breakdown

Output on MST: getChainId() returns 2001. isMST() returns true.

Why: The global variable block.chainid is an EVM opcode (CHAINID) that returns the chain identifier configured in the node. On MST mainnet, the chain ID is 2001. On Ethereum Sepolia, it would be 11155111. The contract itself has no chain-specific code - it simply reads the environment variable. Because MST is fully EVM-compatible, the CHAINID opcode works identically; the only difference is the value returned, which is determined by the node configuration, not the contract bytecode.

Marks breakdown (4 marks):
• 1 mark: Correctly states that getChainId() returns 2001 on MST.
• 1 mark: Correctly states that isMST() returns true.
• 1 mark: Explains that block.chainid is an EVM opcode that reads the node's configured chain ID.
• 1 mark: Explains that EVM compatibility ensures the opcode works identically; the value is environment-dependent, not contract-dependent.

questions.text: "A smart contract that uses OpenZeppelin's ERC-721 implementation and has been audited on Ethereum will require a new security audit after being deployed unchanged on MST."
questions.explanation: 1 mark: Correctly states FALSE and explains that the bytecode and EVM environment are identical, so the audit's findings remain valid.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: Correctly states FALSE and explains that the bytecode and EVM environment are identical, so the audit's findings remain valid.
